metric_value: Declined from approximately 87,000 sites (1992) to projected 42,000 sites (1996) — decline of more than 50% in four years
notes: Source: IBM Corporation, August 1996; decline rate over 20% per year

metric_value: Over 90% of System/36 sites that migrated in 1995 moved to AS/400 solutions — mainly the Advanced 36
notes: Source: IBM Corporation; validates Aberdeen's recommendation to migrate to Advanced 36

metric_value: Installed base declining at a rate in excess of 20% per year; even third-parties finding support less profitable
source_page: Why System/36 Owners Want to Migrate Now
notes: Aberdeen interviews with System/36 owners confirmed declining support ecosystem

metric_value: Hardware: $24,125; Systems software: $9,690; Basic accounting package: ~$12,500 (additional)
notes: Source: Aberdeen Group, September 1996; total minimum ~$46,315 excluding application customization

metric_value: Hardware: $40,875; Systems software: $15,040; Basic accounting package: $22,500
notes: Total ~$78,415 minimum; Aberdeen: System/36 owners should count on at least $50,000 for basic hardware and applications software

metric_name: PC-LAN operational cost rule of thumb
metric_value: $10,000 per year to manage, maintain, and upgrade a Windows NT Server + $1,000 per year for each attached PC
notes: Industry rule-of-thumb from MIS executive interviews; Aberdeen noted companies consistently underestimate PC-LAN maintenance budgets

metric_value: $9,800 to $21,000 depending on model and additional peripherals; all existing System/36 applications run without modification
notes: Source: IBM, September 1996; IBM sometimes offered trade-in allowances for System/36s

metric_value: Maintenance reduced from ~$330/month for larger System/36s to ~$95/month for even larger Advanced 36s (including internal storage)
notes: Approximately 71% maintenance cost reduction; Advanced 36 also used less electricity

metric_value: Advanced 36 runs batch processing jobs 8-10 times faster than System/36 — described as System/36 on steroids
notes: Performance improvement enabled reduction in off-hours MIS personnel costs

metric_value: Average of one day to migrate from System/36 to Advanced 36 using IBM Transition Data Link (TDL); TDL transfers data at 45MB per hour
notes: Maximum reported: 3 days for inexperienced reseller; migration service ~$50/hour

metric_value: Entry level AS/400 around $8,000; initially comparable to Advanced 36 but may need larger configuration due to SSP Environment performance degradation
notes: Performance degradation when running SSP Environment on AS/400 often required larger/more expensive configuration

metric_value: AS/400 can function as application server, PC server (with data auto-inserted into PC spreadsheets), printer server, fax server, Internet gateway, and data warehouse
source_page: Migrate to the Traditional AS/400 — Summary
notes: AS/400 evolution from pure midrange to multi-function server was significant by 1996

metric_value: OS/400 Version 3 Release 6 and Version 3 Release 7 are Year 2000 compliant; growing number of AS/400 applications also Year 2000 compliant
source_page: Migrate to the Traditional AS/400 — Summary
notes: Y2K compliance was a major driver for migration urgency in 1996

metric_value: Most System/36 applications will become dysfunctional well before January 1, 2000 — forecasting and planning use future dates starting years ahead; SSP Year 2000 compliant apps very hard to find
notes: Aberdeen: question of migration is no longer 'should I' but 'when'; Y2K was effectively forcing migration

metric_value: Priority #1 for System/36 owners; Advanced 36 rated Best, AS/400 Acceptable, PC-LAN Unacceptable
notes: Criteria 1 of 5 migration decision framework

metric_value: Priority #2; Advanced 36 rated Best, AS/400 Acceptable, PC-LAN Unacceptable
notes: Criteria 2 of 5; Advanced 36 maintenance $95/month vs System/36 $330/month

metric_name: Migration criteria 3: Lowest MIS transition cost
metric_value: Priority #3; Advanced 36 rated Best (1-day migration via TDL), AS/400 Acceptable, PC-LAN Unacceptable (cannot reuse any S/36 skills or data)
notes: Criteria 3 of 5; PC-LAN required retraining all staff, rewriting all applications, converting all data

metric_name: Migration criteria 4: Least disruptive to business operations
metric_value: Priority #4; Advanced 36 rated Best (same apps, same users, max 3-day cutover), AS/400 Acceptable, PC-LAN Unacceptable (all new processes)
notes: Criteria 4 of 5; zero retraining needed for Advanced 36

metric_value: Priority #5; AS/400 rated Best (multi-billion dollar IBM division), Advanced 36 Acceptable (IBM direct support), PC-LAN Unacceptable (local dealers, hourly billing, no accountability)
notes: Criteria 5 of 5; only criterion where Advanced 36 did not rate Best

metric_value: Advanced 36 is clearly the best choice for the vast majority of current System/36 owners; all former System/36 owners interviewed who chose Advanced 36 were extremely happy
notes: Strong endorsement supported by cost data, field interviews, and IBM migration statistics

metric_name: Aberdeen PC-LAN assessment for legacy migration
metric_value: PC-LAN migration may be appropriate for those automating for the first time; it is not practical for System/36 owners who cannot afford to build new IT infrastructure from scratch
notes: PC-LAN environment described as relatively immature from production MIS viewpoint

metric_value: Majority of System/36 owners prefer NOT to migrate due to high satisfaction with stable platform; yet universally say 'it is time to move' due to external support ecosystem collapse
source_page: Why System/36 Owners Want to Migrate Now
notes: Classic technology lifecycle paradox: high user satisfaction but forced migration due to vendor/ISV abandonment

metric_name: Advanced 36 as transitional bridge platform prediction
metric_value: Aberdeen recommends migrating to Advanced 36 within 12 months then planning for eventual move to OS/400 for Year 2000 compliant applications
notes: Two-phase migration strategy: Advanced 36 now, OS/400 later

metric_name: Advanced 36 transitional bridge platform — actual outcome
metric_value: IBM withdrew Advanced 36 from marketing by 2000; Advanced/36 Machine support removed in OS/400 V4R5; System/36 owners who migrated to Advanced 36 then migrated to AS/400 as predicted; prediction proved accurate
notes: Aberdeen's two-phase migration recommendation proved prescient; Advanced 36 served as bridge exactly as predicted

metric_value: AS/400 division is IBM's most profitable hardware division; IBM will be supporting AS/400 for a significant time to come; resellers and software developers still working on new products
notes: Aberdeen's assessment of AS/400 long-term viability

metric_value: AS/400 evolved to iSeries (2000), System i (2006), IBM Power Systems running IBM i (2008-present); still actively used and developed in 2026 — 30+ years after this prediction; prediction proved highly accurate
notes: One of the most accurate predictions in this study corpus; AS/400/IBM i platform has remarkable longevity

metric_value: Most System/36 applications will become dysfunctional well before January 1, 2000 due to two-digit year fields and forecasting date ranges
notes: Y2K was a credible and real threat to System/36 SSP applications

metric_value: Y2K compliance deadline proved real; System/36 installed base dropped to near zero by 2000; IBM's 90%+ migration rate to AS/400 solutions validated both the urgency and Aberdeen's migration recommendation
notes: System/36 was effectively extinct as production platform by 2000; Y2K pressure was genuine forcing function

metric_value: Advanced 36 detects potential problems before they occur and automatically reports to system manager requesting IBM service call scheduling
notes: Proactive remote diagnostics was a significant capability advantage over older System/36

metric_value: System/36 hardware design and operating system underlying architecture is over 20 years old; has natural limitations compared to more modern systems
notes: System/36 introduced 1983; architecture roots in System/34 (1977) and System/32 (1975); Aberdeen's 20-year claim was approximately correct
